content:
Hi, I noticed a documentation error with soundness implications in Opaque<T> here:

No uniqueness for mutable references: it is fine to have multiple &mut Opaque<T> point to the same value.

This is incorrect; it's always Undefined Behavior to have multiple live &mut references pointing to the same value, regardless of the type of the value. The other points in the same documentation correctly describe the assumptions that are removed by Opaque's use of UnsafeCell and MaybeUninit, so I think the only direct documentation change needed is to remove that one bullet point and changing "all" to "most of" on line 257 above it.

However, if there's other Rust code that has UB from following the incorrect documentation, that'll need to be fixed too, which is why I'm mentioning this here instead of just submitting a patch for the documentation. Also, I haven't contributed to the kernel before and I'm not sure on the process for this subsystem—where would be the right place to submit a patch for this? Thanks.
It's not immediate UB IIUC in practice.
We are using it similar to https://rust-lang.github.io/rfcs/3467-unsafe-pinned.html

The documentation for UnsafeCell seems to say otherwise:

Note that only the immutability guarantee for shared references is affected by UnsafeCell. The uniqueness guarantee for mutable references is unaffected. There is no legal way to obtain aliasing &mut, not even with UnsafeCell<T>.

If I understand correctly, with the usage of UnsafePinned in that RFC, the mutable access is still ultimately done through *mut raw pointers (which can alias), not through &mut references. So, what this means for Rust code using Opaque<T> is that memory access to the stored data needs to be via the provided Opaque::get API (which gives a *mut T raw pointer), not through &mut references.
This means, in particular, that &mut UnsafePinned is not necessarily a unique pointer, and thus the compiler cannot just make aliasing assumptions.

Daniel Hast said:

The documentation for UnsafeCell seems to say otherwise:

Note that only the immutability guarantee for shared references is affected by UnsafeCell. The uniqueness guarantee for mutable references is unaffected. There is no legal way to obtain aliasing &mut, not even with UnsafeCell<T>.

If I understand correctly, with the usage of UnsafePinned in that RFC, the mutable access is still ultimately done through *mut raw pointers (which can alias), not through &mut references. So, what this means for Rust code using Opaque<T> is that memory access to the stored data needs to be via the provided Opaque::get API (which gives a *mut T raw pointer), not through &mut references.

So basically, we need UnsafePinned, but it's not ready yet. However in practice, UnsafeCell<!Unpin> behaves like a UnsafePinned, hence we implement Opaque in this way.

Oh, I see what you're saying, so the idea is that Opaque would act like UnsafePinned? I don't think that avoids UB, though, since Opaque doesn't actually use UnsafePinned (which isn't actually implemented yet), and I'm pretty sure it would require special handling in by the compiler for aliasing &mut references to it not to be UB.
The special handling, in practice right now, is that UnsafeCell<!Unpin> ;-)
and the generated llvm ir won't put noalias for &mut Opaque<!Unpin>.

Here's a snippet in the Rust Playground that shows it still produces UB according to Miri: https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=nightly&mode=debug&edition=2024&gist=cb84850fd6ab2b9a6b6e640adfa2dba2
So even if the generated LLVM IR currently doesn't put noalias on it, it's still UB at the level of Rust's memory model and it could be broken, for example, by MIR optimizations or other compiler changes without warning. (To be clear, the actual code might be fine, as long as it's using raw pointers for access as provided by the Opaque::get method.)

The code you shared doesn't compile:

--> src/main.rs:15:16
|
14 | let ref1 = &mut x;
| ------ first mutable borrow occurs here
15 | let ref2 = &mut x;
| ^^^^^^ second mutable borrow occurs here
16 | black_box(ref1);
| ---- first borrow later used here
https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=nightly&mode=debug&edition=2024&gist=322ec3d2ff984009f6c6a7a77814272a
And Miri passed for the above code snippet.
In other words, you cannot safely get two &mut Opaque<T>, but if you do that unsafely, compilers will generate code without any aliasing assumption, and it's aligned with Rust memory model.

Oops, I forgot to pass through raw pointers there, good point. I'm surprised Miri accepts that, but I guess you're right and it's actually fine. I really thought that was UB but it looks like the memory model properly accounts for that now. Thanks for the clarification!
No problem, thanks for looking into this!

No, wait, it's still UB according to Miri, it's just that we need an actual write (not just black_box) to observe it: https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=nightly&mode=debug&edition=2024&gist=057318412569ae86d561615eb4c61afa
Never mind, that's aliasing the wrong thing, still looks fine.

so yes, that's UB because it derives two &mut i32 from the same address. And that's out of the hands of Opaque<T>, so the correct code should not create these &mut i32 but directly access the data with place expressions.

https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=nightly&mode=debug&edition=2024&gist=19b0a74c1eee45b8f62e8e68534293f8

Yeah, we are cheating a bit, but IIRC we discussed it with upstream Rust at some point, so I think they are aware. We also list it at https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/354. So it should be fine.

Of course, if that assumption changes, then we will definitely need to adapt, but that should be easy, even if we need different code inside Opaque for different compilers.

yeah upstream rust explicitly has the !Unpin hack for self referential futures that we rely on here.
there is an rfc https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/pull/3467 that makes this behavior explicit
